chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing urinary tract infections (UTIs) in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing culture-first diagnostics, appropriate antibiotic use, and follow-up care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of a culture-first approach in diagnosing UTIs in SAM.
Learn appropriate specimen collection methods for urinalysis and culture in children.
Identify empiric antibiotic regimens based on illness severity and local antibiograms.
Recognize the need for follow-up and reassessment in cases of treatment failure or recurrent UTIs.
key_facts: In SAM, UTI should be considered in cases of fever without focus, persistent hypothermia, vomiting, or poor weight gain.
Urine culture should be obtained before starting antibiotics; use suprapubic aspiration, catheterization, or midstream clean-catch methods.
Empiric antibiotics: oral cephalosporin (e.g., cefixime) or amoxicillin/clavulanate for stable children; parenteral cefotaxime or ceftriaxone for toxic or vomiting children.
Treatment duration is 7–10 days unless culture results suggest otherwise.
Aminoglycosides require renal safety monitoring, with doses withheld in cases of oliguria or anuria.
Reassess within 48–72 hours if no improvement, and investigate for urinary obstruction, renal anomalies, or alternative diagnoses.
reference_quote: Obtain urine for urinalysis and culture before antibiotics whenever feasible. Use a properly collected specimen: suprapubic aspiration or catheterization in non-toilet-trained children, and midstream clean-catch in toilet-trained children. Do not use bag urine for culture interpretation in infants and young children. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics, Standard Treatment Guidelines: Urinary tract infection in children)
document_key_topics: Culture-first approach in UTI management for SAM
Empiric antibiotic regimens for pediatric UTIs
Specimen collection methods for urinalysis and culture
Follow-up and reassessment in UTI treatment failure
